perturbation.original_text: 5. Title. Title or the right to possess Confidential Information as between the parties will remain in the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'Title or the right to possess Confidential Information as between the parties will remain in the disclosing party.' it creates uncertainty regarding the ownership of the Confidential Information after disclosure. Without stating that the title remains with the disclosing party, it could be interpreted that the receiving party gains some rights to the information. This contradicts the core nature of a non-disclosure agreement. This in-text contradiction arises within the context of the agreement's overall purpose, which is to protect the disclosing party's confidential information, and it's also contradicted in Page 10, Section 6 of the CITR, where it states: 'no license under any patent, copyright, trade secret or other intellectual property right is granted to or conferred upon either party in this Agreement'
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. Both parties understand and acknowledge that no license under any patent, copyright, trade secret or other intellectual property right is granted to or conferred upon either party in this Agreement or by the disclosure of any Confidential Information by one party to the other party as contemplated hereunder, either expressly, by implication, inducement, estoppel or otherwise, and that any license under such intellectual property rights must be express and in writing.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly stated that 'Title or the right to possess Confidential Information as between the parties will remain in the disclosing party.' The changed text omits this statement entirely, creating uncertainty about the ownership of confidential information. This omission contradicts Section 6, which states 'no license under any patent, copyright, trade secret or other intellectual property right is granted to or conferred upon either party in this Agreement...'. The original text aligned with the intent of section 6, but the removal of that text creates an in-text contradiction by implying that the receiving party might gain some rights, conflicting with the explicit denial of any such rights in Section 6.

perturbation.original_text: 6. No Obligation of Disclosure: Termination. Neither party has any obligation to disclose Confidential Information to the other. Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon written notice to the other party: provided that each party's obligations with respect to Confidential Information disclosed during the term of this Agreement will survive any such termination. Either party may, at any time: (a) cease giving Confidential Information to the other party without any liability and/or (b) request in writing the return or destruction of all or part of its Confidential Information previously disclosed, and all copies thereof, and the receiving party will promptly comply with such request, and certify in writing its compliance.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. No Obligation of Disclosure: Termination. Neither party has any obligation to disclose Confidential Information to the other. Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon written notice to the other party. Either party may, at any time: (a) cease giving Confidential Information to the other party without any liability and/or (b) request in writing the return or destruction of all or part of its Confidential Information previously disclosed, and all copies thereof, and the receiving party will promptly comply with such request, and certify in writing its compliance.
perturbation.explanation: The critical clause stating that 'each party's obligations with respect to Confidential Information disclosed during the term of this Agreement will survive any such termination' has been removed. This creates an in-text contradiction with Section 3 in the CITR (Page 9): 'This CITR incorporates all the terms and conditions of the Corporate Non-Disclosure Agreement ("CNDA") executed by the parties.' Section 3 mentions that the CNDA (this agreement) survives, while Section 6 of the original document no longer mentions the CNDA's survivability, thus creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Unless a shorter period is indicated below, the disclosing party will not assert any claims of breach or misappropriation of trade secrets against the receiving party arising from the receiving party's disclosure of the disclosing party's Confidential Information under this CITR more than five (5) years from the date when such information was disclosed. However, unless at least one of the exceptions set forth in Section 4 of the CNDA has occurred, the receiving party will continue to treat such Confidential Information as the confidential information of the disclosing party and only disclose any such Confidential Information to third parties under the terms of a non-disclosure agreement. Either party may at any time request in writing the immediate return of all or part of its Confidential Information disclosed hereunder, and all copies thereof, and the receiving party shall promptly comply with such request.
perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause 'each party's obligations with respect to Confidential Information disclosed during the term of this Agreement will survive any such termination' from Section 6 in the Changed Text directly contradicts the Contradicted Text, Section 3, which incorporates the CNDA (the agreement being modified). Section 3 refers to the ongoing obligations concerning confidential information even after termination, contingent on certain exceptions. The removal of the survivability clause in Section 6 makes the continued obligation in Section 3 contradictory because the CNDA, as modified, no longer explicitly supports such survival after termination, thereby creating a strong in-text contradiction. Without the clause, the receiving party's obligation to treat Confidential Information as confidential after termination is weakened or negated, directly conflicting with Section 3.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Residuals. Notwithstanding anything herein to the contrary, either party may use Residuals for any purpose, including without limitation use in development manufacture, promotion, sale and maintenance of its products or services: provided that this right to Residuals does not represent a licence under any patents, copyrights or other intellectual property rights of the disclosing party. The term "Residuals" means any information retained in the unaided memories of the receiving party's employees who have had access to the disclosing party's Confidential Information pursuant to the terms of this Agreement. An employee's memory is unaided if the employee has not intentionally memorized the Confidential Information for the purpose of retaining and subsequently using or disclosing it.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Residuals. Notwithstanding anything herein to the contrary, either party may use Residuals for any purpose, including without limitation use in development manufacture, promotion, sale and maintenance of its products or services: provided that this right to Residuals does not represent a licence under any patents, copyrights or other intellectual property rights of the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the definition of 'Residuals' i.e. 'The term "Residuals" means any information retained in the unaided memories of the receiving party's employees who have had access to the disclosing party's Confidential Information pursuant to the terms of this Agreement. An employee's memory is unaided if the employee has not intentionally memorized the Confidential Information for the purpose of retaining and subsequently using or disclosing it.' it creates ambiguity, making enforcement difficult. Addendum No. 1, Section 2 discusses residuals, stating that Intel can use residuals of the Confidential Information, thus creating an in-text contradiction with the removal of the residuals definition in the original document.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. Section 7 Residuals is hereby deleted and replaced with the following: The Company understands that Intel is a diverse corporation which conducts research and development activities in an immense variety of technologies, often resulting in new commercial product development. The Company acknowledges that Intel may already be working on similar technology as that disclosed by the Company, and that Intel's personnel to whom the disclosure is made may be wholly unaware of this work. Intel's receipt of Confidential Information under this agreement shall not create any obligation in any way limiting, restricting, or prohibiting Intel's assignment of employees or contractors. Notwithstanding anything herein to the contrary, Intel may use residuals of the Confidential Information for any purpose including without limitation use in development, manufacture, promotion, sale and maintenance of Intel's products and services. The term "residuals" as used herein means any information relating to the Company's technology retained in the unaided memories of Intel's Representatives who have had access to such Confidential Information pursuant to the terms of this agreement. A Representative's <PAGE> 5 memory is unaided if the Representative has not intentionally memorized the Confidential Information for the purpose of retaining and subsequently using or disclosing it. This provision grants no patent or copyright license.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Residuals,' which is then used in Addendum No. 1, Section 2. The changed text removes the definition of 'Residuals,' creating ambiguity. Addendum No. 1, Section 2 refers to 'residuals of the Confidential Information', and Intel can use these for any purpose. The removal of the definition creates a direct conflict with the contradicted section which uses the term, thus making the agreement difficult to enforce since the meaning of 'Residuals' is no longer provided in Section 7, as it was originally.

perturbation.original_text: (d) This Agreement will be governed by laws of the State of Delaware without reference to conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) This Agreement will be governed by laws of State without reference to conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'of the State of Delaware' it creates ambiguity as to which State's laws will govern the agreement. This contradicts the explicit choice of Delaware law provided in the original text. This In-Text Contradiction, is further aggravated in Page 9, Section 5 of the CITR where it claims all the terms and conditions in the CNDA are in full effect; thus creating a contradiction where no specific state law is indicated on one end, but then claims the CNDA is in full force where it stipulates Delaware law.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. All other terms and conditions of the executed CNDA shall remain in full force and effect. Nothing contained herein shall be construed as amending or modifying the terms of the CNDA referenced above.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that Delaware law governs the agreement. The changed text removes 'of the State of Delaware', creating ambiguity about which state's laws apply. This directly contradicts section 5, which states that all terms and conditions of the CNDA (which presumably specifies Delaware law as per the original text) remain in full force and effect, thus creating an in-text contradiction. Because the change introduces ambiguity where the contradicted clause expects certainty, it breaks the compatibility, thereby creating a strong contradiction.
